PFASGroups Fingerprint Tanimoto Benchmark

Generated: 2026-03-08 01:55  |  Phase-1 compounds: 40  |  Phase-2 compounds: 18  |  Phase-2B compounds: 18  |  FP configs tested: 39 (14 graph metrics, 10 mol metrics)

Phase-1 compound series (11 chemical families): PFAA chain C2–C11 (10), PFSA C4/C6/C8/C10 (4), iso-PFCA alpha/beta-branch (6), iso-PFSA (2), HFPO-DA / GenX (1), FTOH 4:2–10:2 (4), FTS 6:2/8:2 (2), FTCA 4:2–8:2 (3) – FTOH scaffold with COOH head, PFPA C4/C6 phosphonic acids (2), iso-heavy gem/double/large-branch (4) – branching degree, FG-eccentric star-topology C5/C7 (2) – FG topological position. Lower mean off-diagonal Tanimoto = better discrimination.
Phase-2 compound set: 18 structurally diverse PFAS present in both the Richard 2023 validation TSV and the TxP-PFAS SI Table S2 CSV. TxP-PFAS (129-bit CSRML) is benchmarked as a competitor. TxP-PFAS mean T on this set: 0.1334
Phase-2B extended set: 18 compounds across 7 families (PFAA, PFSA, Telomer, Sulfonamide, Branched, PFPA, Ether-PFAS) – PFASGroups only, no TxP-PFAS CSV required.

Phase 1 – Fingerprint Selection

1.0 Phase-1 compound structures

The 40 compounds span 11 structural series chosen to challenge each fingerprint on: (a) chain-length homologues (PFAA C2–C11, PFSA C4–C10), (b) branching position (iso-PFCA α/β) and degree (iso-heavy: gem-CF₃, double-CF₃, C₂F₅-branch), (c) functional-group variation at constant component size (FTCA vs FTOH vs FTS; PFPA vs PFCA vs PFSA), (d) topological FG position / eccentricity (FG-eccentric: star vs. linear).

40 Phase-1 compounds – 6 per row

1.1 All configs ranked by discrimination

1.2 Per-metric impact relative to binary baseline

1.3 Tanimoto heatmaps (binary + top 8)

1.4 MDS projections (binary + top 6)

How to read MDS plots: Multi-Dimensional Scaling (MDS) converts the pairwise Tanimoto distance matrix (1 − similarity) into a 2-D scatter plot while preserving inter-point distances as faithfully as possible. Two compounds placed close together means the fingerprint cannot distinguish them; two compounds far apart means the fingerprint sees them as structurally different.
  • Series separation – each chemical series (PFAA, PFSA, FTOH…) should form a compact, well-separated cluster; overlap between series = lost discrimination.
  • Chain-length ordering – within a homologous series the points should be roughly collinear with C-number increasing monotonically along the axis.
  • Branching resolution – iso-PFCA / iso-PFSA variants should sit away from their linear homologues, not merged with them.
  • Fingerprint comparison (Phase 2) – a panel where all 18 compounds are spread far apart is more discriminating than one where everything clusters in the centre; this correlates directly with a low mean off-diagonal Tanimoto.

1.5 Per-pair similarity for top-20 configs

1.6 Per-group GSI breakdown

This chart decomposes the overall Group Separation Index into per-series contributions. A config that scores high globally but only for one series may be less generically useful than one that scores more evenly across all series.

Phase 2 – Discrimination benchmark vs TxP-PFAS

TxP-PFAS (129-bit CSRML, Richard 2023) is included as a competitor. Lower mean T = more discriminating fingerprint.

2.0 Phase-2 compound structures

The 18 compounds were selected as the overlap between the Richard 2023 validation set and the TxP-PFAS SI Table S2 (14 735 compounds).

18 Phase-2 compounds – 6 per row

2.1 Pairwise Tanimoto heatmaps (top-5 PFASGroups configs + TxP-PFAS)

2.2 Discrimination comparison (mean T, lower = better)

2.3 MDS projections

How to read MDS plots: Multi-Dimensional Scaling (MDS) converts the pairwise Tanimoto distance matrix (1 − similarity) into a 2-D scatter plot while preserving inter-point distances as faithfully as possible. Two compounds placed close together means the fingerprint cannot distinguish them; two compounds far apart means the fingerprint sees them as structurally different.
  • Series separation – each chemical series (PFAA, PFSA, FTOH…) should form a compact, well-separated cluster; overlap between series = lost discrimination.
  • Chain-length ordering – within a homologous series the points should be roughly collinear with C-number increasing monotonically along the axis.
  • Branching resolution – iso-PFCA / iso-PFSA variants should sit away from their linear homologues, not merged with them.
  • Fingerprint comparison (Phase 2) – a panel where all 18 compounds are spread far apart is more discriminating than one where everything clusters in the centre; this correlates directly with a low mean off-diagonal Tanimoto.

2.4 Per-group GSI breakdown (PFASGroups configs vs TxP-PFAS)

Answers: "Is TxP-PFAS's higher overall GSI driven by just one well-separated group (e.g. FT surfactant), or does it broadly outperform?" If only one group's bar is taller for TxP-PFAS (red, black outline) while others are comparable or lower, the global GSI advantage is group-specific, not general.

Phase 2B – Extended compound set (18 compounds, PFASGroups only)

Phase 2B tests discrimination on a larger, more diverse set of 18 compounds spanning 7 chemical families (PFAA, PFSA, Telomer, Sulfonamide, Branched, PFPA, Ether-PFAS). No TxP-PFAS CSV is needed. This section assesses how fingerprint discrimination scales with more compound families, and identifies which families remain hard to separate.
